title: Orbital Cellulitis
oneLiner: Infection posterior to the orbital septum causing proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and fever; an ophthalmological and neurosurgical emergency needing immediate IV antibiotics and imaging.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST):** Bilateral periorbital oedema, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V₁, VI), high fever, headache, seizures — do NOT miss if contralateral eye becomes involved or new focal neurology.
- **Meningitis/intracranial abscess:** Neck stiffness, photophobia, positive Kernig’s, GCS <15 — immediate CT head + LP (after CT) if suspected.
- **Central retinal artery occlusion (from septic embolus):** Sudden painless vision loss + afferent pupillary defect (APD) — urgent ophthalmology and vascular assessment.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms (you will see):**  
  - Unilateral periorbital swelling, erythema, warmth  
  - Fever (often ≥38°C)  
  - Pain on eye movement (hallmark)  
  - Proptosis (visible forward displacement)  
  - Reduced or painful eye movements  
  - Diplopia  
  - Decreased visual acuity (late sign – alarming)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart) – document baseline  
  - Pupils: APD suggests optic nerve compromise  
  - Extraocular movements: restriction + pain in any direction  
  - Conjunctival chemosis (oedema)  
  - Proptosis: measure with Hertel exophthalmometer if available; otherwise gross inspection  
  - Fundoscopy: disc swelling, venous stasis, retinal haemorrhages
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis:** No proptosis, no ophthalmoplegia, pain-free eye movement, normal vision — **splitter:** proptosis + pain on movement = orbital, not preseptal.
- **Allergic lid oedema:** Bilateral, non‑tender, no fever, no restriction.
- **Orbital tumour (e.g., lymphoma, rhabdomyosarcoma):** Painless, chronic, no fever.
- **Traumatic haematoma/emphysema:** History of trauma, no fever.
- **Endophthalmitis:** Painful, but no proptosis; intraocular inflammation on slit‑lamp.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Snellen visual acuity (each eye separately)  
  - Pupils + APD (swinging flashlight test)  
  - Confrontational visual fields  
  - Eye movement assessment  
  - Fundoscopy
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, glucose  
  - Blood cultures ×2 (before antibiotics)  
  - Coagulation screen if CNS involvement suspected
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First‑line:** CT orbits with contrast (thin slices, axial + coronal) – shows fat stranding, abscess, sinusitis, bony erosion.  
  - **Second‑line:** MRI orbits + brain with gadolinium (if CT contraindicated or intracerebral extension suspected).
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lumbar puncture (after CT) if meningitis suspected  
  - Nasal endoscopy by ENT (identifies source, guides drainage)
- **Never‑miss test:** **CT orbits with contrast** – omission delays definitive diagnosis and can cost vision or life.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Urgent ENT + ophthalmology consultation (both)  
  - IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (start within 1 hour of seeing patient)  
  - Analgesia (paracetamol ± NSAIDs if not contraindicated)  
  - Eye care: lubricating drops (e.g., hypromellose), eye shield if corneal exposure  
  - Keep nil‑by‑mouth (NBM) if surgical drainage anticipated
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV antibiotics (7–10 days typical)  
  - Monitor visual acuity, pupils, eye movements every 4 hours (document)  
  - NEWS2 chart; escalate if deteriorating  
  - Nasal decongestants (e.g., xylometazoline 0.1% spray, 2 puffs BD) if sinusitis  
  - Surgical drainage if: orbital abscess on CT, no improvement after 48h, new vision loss, worsening proptosis
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - Switch to oral antibiotics (guided by cultures) after clinical response  
  - Complete 2–3 week course  
  - ENT follow‑up for sinus source management  
  - Ophthalmology follow‑up for visual rehabilitation if needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1L/8h if septic (temporarily; review daily)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if eGFR ≥30 mL/min; adjust if low)  
  - Diet: NBM until ENT/ophthalmology decide on surgery; then light diet  
  - Observations: NEWS2 q1h for first 6h, then q4h if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agents (UK practice):**  
  - *Co‑amoxiclav:* 1.2g IV 8‑hourly – covers Gram‑positives, anaerobes, *Haemophilus influenzae*, sinus pathogens.  
  - *Ceftriaxone:* 2g IV once daily (alternative if penicillin allergy or severe infection) ± *metronidazole:* 500mg IV TDS (add for anaerobic cover).
  - *Vancomycin* (15–20 mg/kg IV 12‑hourly, check trough levels) if MRSA risk.
- **Duration:** IV until afebrile + clinically improved (typically 7–10 days), then oral switch (e.g., co‑amoxiclav 625mg PO TDS total 2–3 weeks).
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy (avoid co‑amoxiclav; use ceftriaxone + metronidazole).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ceftriaxone dose adjustment not usually needed; but vancomycin needs trough monitoring.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting IV antibiotics without first obtaining blood cultures** (one missed culture can deprive you of targeted therapy and sensitivity data). Also: do not give broad‑spectrum antibiotics without checking renal function – if using gentamicin (uncommon first line now) must check levels before second dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Vision loss (optic nerve ischaemia/compression)  
  - Cavernous sinus thrombosis  
  - Meningitis, brain abscess  
  - Sepsis / septic shock  
  - Corneal ulceration (from exposure)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Persistent diplopia (due to fibrosis of extraocular muscles)  
  - Enophthalmos (after abscess drained)  
  - Chronic sinusitis
sections.Prognosis: - **Mortality** ~5–10% with timely treatment; **vision loss** ~10–20% (higher if treatment delayed >24h). Full recovery common if antibiotics + surgery are given early.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document visual acuity and pupil reaction before starting antibiotics** – this is your baseline for escalation.  
- **Call ENT and ophthalmology simultaneously** – both are needed (ENT for sinus drainage, ophthalmology for orbital decompression).  
- **If proptosis or ophthalmoplegia is present, never discharge** – the patient must be admitted for IV antibiotics and observation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT mistake for preseptal cellulitis and give oral antibiotics alone** – orbital involvement requires IV therapy and imaging.  
- **Do NOT give steroids early** – may mask a developing abscess or fungal infection; use only after source control.  
- **Do NOT discharge with oral antibiotics without reviewing imaging** – orbital abscess can progress silently.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home — E:
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening vision (blurring, double vision)  
  - New headache, fever, or eye pain  
  - Any new neurological symptoms (slurred speech, limb weakness, seizure)  
- **Follow‑up:** Ophthalmology clinic within 24–48h; ENT clinic for sinus source evaluation.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) + ophthalmology + ENT urgently if:**  
  - New visual loss or APD  
  - GCS drops (<15)  
  - Bilateral eye involvement  
  - Fever persisting >72h despite antibiotics  
  - No improvement after 48h of IV antibiotics  
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - Septic shock (hypotension, vasopressor needed)  
  - Depressed level of consciousness (GCS <9)

rawMarkdown:
## Orbital Cellulitis
**One-liner:** Infection posterior to the orbital septum causing proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and fever; an ophthalmological and neurosurgical emergency needing immediate IV antibiotics and imaging.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cavernous sinus thrombosis (CST):** Bilateral periorbital oedema, cranial nerve palsies (III, IV, V₁, VI), high fever, headache, seizures — do NOT miss if contralateral eye becomes involved or new focal neurology.
- **Meningitis/intracranial abscess:** Neck stiffness, photophobia, positive Kernig’s, GCS <15 — immediate CT head + LP (after CT) if suspected.
- **Central retinal artery occlusion (from septic embolus):** Sudden painless vision loss + afferent pupillary defect (APD) — urgent ophthalmology and vascular assessment.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms (you will see):**  
  - Unilateral periorbital swelling, erythema, warmth  
  - Fever (often ≥38°C)  
  - Pain on eye movement (hallmark)  
  - Proptosis (visible forward displacement)  
  - Reduced or painful eye movements  
  - Diplopia  
  - Decreased visual acuity (late sign – alarming)
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Visual acuity (Snellen chart) – document baseline  
  - Pupils: APD suggests optic nerve compromise  
  - Extraocular movements: restriction + pain in any direction  
  - Conjunctival chemosis (oedema)  
  - Proptosis: measure with Hertel exophthalmometer if available; otherwise gross inspection  
  - Fundoscopy: disc swelling, venous stasis, retinal haemorrhages

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Preseptal (periorbital) cellulitis:** No proptosis, no ophthalmoplegia, pain-free eye movement, normal vision — **splitter:** proptosis + pain on movement = orbital, not preseptal.
- **Allergic lid oedema:** Bilateral, non‑tender, no fever, no restriction.
- **Orbital tumour (e.g., lymphoma, rhabdomyosarcoma):** Painless, chronic, no fever.
- **Traumatic haematoma/emphysema:** History of trauma, no fever.
- **Endophthalmitis:** Painful, but no proptosis; intraocular inflammation on slit‑lamp.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**  
  - Snellen visual acuity (each eye separately)  
  - Pupils + APD (swinging flashlight test)  
  - Confrontational visual fields  
  - Eye movement assessment  
  - Fundoscopy
- **Bloods:**  
  - FBC, CRP, U&Es, glucose  
  - Blood cultures ×2 (before antibiotics)  
  - Coagulation screen if CNS involvement suspected
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First‑line:** CT orbits with contrast (thin slices, axial + coronal) – shows fat stranding, abscess, sinusitis, bony erosion.  
  - **Second‑line:** MRI orbits + brain with gadolinium (if CT contraindicated or intracerebral extension suspected).
- **Special tests:**  
  - Lumbar puncture (after CT) if meningitis suspected  
  - Nasal endoscopy by ENT (identifies source, guides drainage)
- **Never‑miss test:** **CT orbits with contrast** – omission delays definitive diagnosis and can cost vision or life.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Urgent ENT + ophthalmology consultation (both)  
  - IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics (start within 1 hour of seeing patient)  
  - Analgesia (paracetamol ± NSAIDs if not contraindicated)  
  - Eye care: lubricating drops (e.g., hypromellose), eye shield if corneal exposure  
  - Keep nil‑by‑mouth (NBM) if surgical drainage anticipated
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Continue IV antibiotics (7–10 days typical)  
  - Monitor visual acuity, pupils, eye movements every 4 hours (document)  
  - NEWS2 chart; escalate if deteriorating  
  - Nasal decongestants (e.g., xylometazoline 0.1% spray, 2 puffs BD) if sinusitis  
  - Surgical drainage if: orbital abscess on CT, no improvement after 48h, new vision loss, worsening proptosis
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - Switch to oral antibiotics (guided by cultures) after clinical response  
  - Complete 2–3 week course  
  - ENT follow‑up for sinus source management  
  - Ophthalmology follow‑up for visual rehabilitation if needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - Fluids: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 1L/8h if septic (temporarily; review daily)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if eGFR ≥30 mL/min; adjust if low)  
  - Diet: NBM until ENT/ophthalmology decide on surgery; then light diet  
  - Observations: NEWS2 q1h for first 6h, then q4h if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agents (UK practice):**  
  - *Co‑amoxiclav:* 1.2g IV 8‑hourly – covers Gram‑positives, anaerobes, *Haemophilus influenzae*, sinus pathogens.  
  - *Ceftriaxone:* 2g IV once daily (alternative if penicillin allergy or severe infection) ± *metronidazole:* 500mg IV TDS (add for anaerobic cover).
  - *Vancomycin* (15–20 mg/kg IV 12‑hourly, check trough levels) if MRSA risk.
- **Duration:** IV until afebrile + clinically improved (typically 7–10 days), then oral switch (e.g., co‑amoxiclav 625mg PO TDS total 2–3 weeks).
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy (avoid co‑amoxiclav; use ceftriaxone + metronidazole).  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Ceftriaxone dose adjustment not usually needed; but vancomycin needs trough monitoring.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting IV antibiotics without first obtaining blood cultures** (one missed culture can deprive you of targeted therapy and sensitivity data). Also: do not give broad‑spectrum antibiotics without checking renal function – if using gentamicin (uncommon first line now) must check levels before second dose.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Vision loss (optic nerve ischaemia/compression)  
  - Cavernous sinus thrombosis  
  - Meningitis, brain abscess  
  - Sepsis / septic shock  
  - Corneal ulceration (from exposure)
- **Chronic:**  
  - Persistent diplopia (due to fibrosis of extraocular muscles)  
  - Enophthalmos (after abscess drained)  
  - Chronic sinusitis

### Prognosis
- **Mortality** ~5–10% with timely treatment; **vision loss** ~10–20% (higher if treatment delayed >24h). Full recovery common if antibiotics + surgery are given early.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always document visual acuity and pupil reaction before starting antibiotics** – this is your baseline for escalation.  
- **Call ENT and ophthalmology simultaneously** – both are needed (ENT for sinus drainage, ophthalmology for orbital decompression).  
- **If proptosis or ophthalmoplegia is present, never discharge** – the patient must be admitted for IV antibiotics and observation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT mistake for preseptal cellulitis and give oral antibiotics alone** – orbital involvement requires IV therapy and imaging.  
- **Do NOT give steroids early** – may mask a developing abscess or fungal infection; use only after source control.  
- **Do NOT discharge with oral antibiotics without reviewing imaging** – orbital abscess can progress silently.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home — Extremely Rare)
- **Return if:**  
  - Worsening vision (blurring, double vision)  
  - New headache, fever, or eye pain  
  - Any new neurological symptoms (slurred speech, limb weakness, seizure)  
- **Follow‑up:** Ophthalmology clinic within 24–48h; ENT clinic for sinus source evaluation.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) + ophthalmology + ENT urgently if:**  
  - New visual loss or APD  
  - GCS drops (<15)  
  - Bilateral eye involvement  
  - Fever persisting >72h despite antibiotics  
  - No improvement after 48h of IV antibiotics  
- **Call ICU/crash team if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or rising  
  - Lactate >2 mmol/L  
  - Septic shock (hypotension, vasopressor needed)  
  - Depressed level of consciousness (GCS <9)
